summary: DeVault argues that the best way to contribute to open source is not to ask 'how can I help?' but to scratch your own itches. He contends that gratitude-driven contributions require more maintainer effort and rarely become recurring. Instead, contributors should be intrinsically motivated by bugs or missing features that personally affect them. He encourages spreading contributions across the entire FOSS ecosystem rather than concentrating on established projects.
key_points: The best contributors are intrinsically motivated by their own needs, not by a desire to give back
Gratitude-driven contributions tend to burden maintainers more and rarely lead to ongoing involvement
Established projects attract more volunteers while newer, incomplete projects that need help most get fewer
Contributors should spread their work across the FOSS ecosystem rather than fixating on one project
The key skill to develop is getting up to speed on unfamiliar codebases quickly
money_quotes: I get this question a lot! The answer is usually… don't.
The best contributors are always intrinsically motivated.
Instead, you should focus on scratching your own itches.
Don't be afraid to work on any free- and open-source codebase that you encounter — nearly all of them will accept your patches.
If we spread the contributions around, the FOSS ecosystem will flourish and the benefits will come back around to our project, too.
Focus on developing skills to get up to speed on new codebases fast.
When you file bug reports with a FOSS project, get into the habit of following up with a patch which addresses the problem.
key_insight: The most valuable open-source contributions come from solving your own problems, not from asking maintainers how you can help.
